Yes.

Reasoning: In this hypothetical world, the only causal relationships are that yupt → muvq and zupj → muvq, and we are told that yupt or zupj causes muvq (i.e., muvq is caused if either yupt or zupj is present). We observe that the individual is not zupj. Therefore, the only possible cause for muvq is yupt. If the individual were yupt (instead of not yupt), then the cause yupt would be present, and since yupt has a direct effect on muvq, muvq would occur. Conversely, if the individual were not yupt (and we already know they are not zupj), then no cause is present, so muvq would not occur. Thus, changing from not yupt to yupt would result in muvq.

Reasoning: In this hypothetical world, the only causes of muvq are yupt and zupj, and we are told that muvq occurs if either yupt or zupj is present. We also know the individual is not zupj. Therefore, whether muvq occurs depends entirely on yupt. If yupt is present, muvq will occur; if yupt is absent, muvq will not occur. Thus, changing yupt from “not yupt” to “yupt” would cause muvq to change from not occurring to occurring.

Yes. In this hypothetical world, muvq is caused by either yupt or zupj. Since the individual is not zupj, the only remaining cause of muvq is yupt. Therefore, if the individual were yupt instead of not yupt, muvq would occur.

Yes. In this hypothetical causal system, muvq is caused by either yupt or zupj, with both having direct effects on muvq. Since the individual is observed to be not zupj, the only remaining potential cause for muvq is yupt. Therefore, if yupt were present instead of absent, it would directly cause muvq, making muvq true for this individual.
